preamble: The substitution or shift of the voiceless dental fricative [θ] to the voiceless post-alveolar affricate [tʃ] (represented here as 'ch') is a form of affrication commonly observed in specific second-language varieties and certain palatalizing environments.

examples.note: Common substitution where the unfamiliar dental fricative is replaced by an acoustically similar affricate already present in the speaker's phonology.
While not a direct θ > tʃ, it illustrates the tendency for dental fricatives to shift toward palatal or post-alveolar places of articulation (e.g., *t > tʃ, *θ > ʃ).
